era_context: The Axial Age in full flourish. Neo-Babylonian Empire consolidating Near Eastern hegemony under Nebuchadnezzar II. Chinese Spring and Autumn period ongoing, with competing states vying for dominance. Greek city-states developing democratic institutions and philosophy. South Asian Vedic culture transitioning toward the Upanishadic period. Iron Age technologies and warfare dominating Eurasia. A period of intellectual ferment and political consolidation across multiple civilisations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe (limited contact with literate civilisations)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited written sources)

events.title: Zedekiah consolidates rule as vassal king of Judah
events.description: Zedekiah, installed as vassal king of Judah by Nebuchadnezzar II following Jehoiachin's deportation (597 BCE), consolidates his rule. He rules as a Babylonian vassal, though his reign is marked by internal tensions and periodic resistance to Babylonian authority. Zedekiah's reign lasts until 586 BCE, when Nebuchadnezzar II besieges and destroys Jerusalem. The specific events of 596 BCE are not well-documented, but the year falls within Zedekiah's period of consolidation.
events.certainty_note: Zedekiah's reign is well-attested in both Babylonian and Hebrew sources. The specific events of 596 BCE are not individually documented; the year falls within his period of rule (597–586 BCE).

events.title: No other major documented events
events.description: No other major documented events are known for 596 BCE. The year is sparsely attested in surviving sources. Broader historical processes (state consolidation, intellectual development, technological diffusion) continued, but no singular event of major historical significance is recorded in the surviving record.
events.certainty_note: The sparsity of documentation for 596 BCE is confirmed; the absence of other major events reflects the limitations of the surviving record.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The sparsity of documentation for 596 BCE makes it difficult to identify disputed claims. The year is largely absent from historical narratives beyond the general context of Zedekiah's reign.
historiographic_note: The year 596 BCE is poorly documented in surviving sources. Babylonian cuneiform records are fragmentary for this specific year. Hebrew biblical sources provide narrative context but are not contemporary. Chinese chronicles (Chunqiu) record the year but provide minimal detail. Greek sources are almost entirely absent. The historical record for this year is essentially a gap, which is typical for the ancient world. Most years before the Common Era lack detailed documentation; only major events (wars, eclipses, regime changes) are typically recorded. The year 596 BCE falls within a period of relative stability in Judah (between Jehoiachin's deportation in 597 BCE and the final destruction of Jerusalem in 586 BCE), which may explain the absence of major documented events.

graph_edges.note: Zedekiah's consolidation follows his installation in 597 BCE
Zedekiah's reign as vassal king ends with the destruction of Jerusalem in 586 BCE
